subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: This guide is written for the teacher or parent who will lead a young child (age 5–6) through Unit 2. Year 1 learners are usually pre-readers, so this unit is taught with you, not read alone by the child. Your job is to show real objects, name them, and play sorting and building games alongside the child. The child's own work happens in the companion Activity Sheet and Practice documents.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 1 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Name common 3D shapes: cube, sphere and cylinder.
Talk about the faces, edges and curved surfaces of a 3D shape.
Say which 3D shapes can roll and which can stack.
Name common 2D shapes: circle, square, rectangle and triangle.
Count the sides of a 2D shape and find its corners.
Tell the difference between straight and curved sides.
Sort shapes into groups by a property (rolls / stacks, straight / curved).
Spot and name shapes in everyday objects at home and outside.
A ball or an orange (a sphere).

concepts.title: Meeting 3D Shapes (cube, sphere, cylinder)
concepts.explanation: Start with real solid objects, not pictures. A 3D shape is one the child can hold in their hand – it takes up space and has depth. Gather three things: a ball (sphere), a tin or tube (cylinder), and a box or die (cube). Let the child pick each one up, turn it over, and feel it before you name it. How to teach it: Hand the child the ball and say its name: ‘This is a sphere. It is round all over.’ Do the same with the cube (‘a box shape with flat sides’) and the cylinder (‘like a tin – flat circles at the ends and a…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Hold the ball. Is it round all over, or does it have flat sides?” Child: “Round all over!” You: “Yes — a shape that is round all over is a sphere.” You: “Now hold the tin. Find the flat round…

concepts.title: Faces, Edges and Curved Surfaces
concepts.explanation: Once the child can name the three shapes, help them describe the parts. A face is a flat part you can lay on the table. An edge is the line where two faces meet. A curved surface is a smooth, rounded part with no corners at all. How to teach it: Give the child a cube and count the flat faces together as you turn it – there are 6, and each one is a square. Run a finger along an edge and say the word. Then give the child the sphere: it has no flat faces and no edges, just one curved surface – that is why it rolls so…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Let us count the flat faces on the cube. Touch each one as we count.” Child and you: “One, two, three, four, five, six.” You: “Six flat faces, and every one is a square. Now feel the ball —…

concepts.title: Rolling and Stacking
concepts.explanation: This is a favourite, because the child can test it. The rule is simple: a shape can roll if it has a curved surface, and a shape can stack if it has flat faces to sit on. How to teach it: Gently tip each shape on the floor or a tray. The sphere rolls everywhere (curved all over). The cube stacks and does not roll (all flat faces). The cylinder does both – it rolls on its curved side and stacks when stood on a flat end. Then build a little tower together and talk about which shape is best at the bottom (a cube – it…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Give the ball a little push. What does it do?” Child: “It rolls!” You: “Why does it roll?” Child: “Because it is round.” You: “Now try the box. Does it roll?” Child: “No, it stays still.”…

concepts.title: Meeting 2D Shapes (circle, square, rectangle, triangle)
concepts.explanation: A 2D shape is flat – you can draw it on paper, but you cannot pick it up off the page. A good way to meet 2D shapes is to draw around the flat faces of the solid objects: draw around the end of the tin to make a circle, or around one face of the box to make a square. How to teach it: Introduce the four shapes one at a time, always with a real example. The circle is round like a plate and has 1 curved side with no corners. The square has 4 straight sides all the same length and 4 corners. The rectangle also has 4…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “This plate is a circle. Trace the edge with your finger — round and round, no corners.” You: “Now the window. Count the sides: one, two, three, four. All the same length — that makes it a…

concepts.title: Counting Sides and Corners; Straight or Curved
concepts.explanation: Now sharpen the child's eye for the properties that tell shapes apart. Counting sides is the key skill. Encourage the child to touch and count each side, and to mark a starting point so they do not go around twice. How to teach it: Lay out the four shapes. Together, touch and count the sides: circle 1 (a single curved side), triangle 3, square 4, rectangle 4. Then sort by side type: the circle has a curved side; the square, rectangle and triangle all have straight sides only. Ask the child to find the corners too…
concepts.example: Two ideas are worth repeating gently. First, turning a shape does not change it: a square tilted to look like a diamond is still a square. Second, triangles come in many shapes – tall thin ones, wide flat ones – and…

concepts.title: Sorting Shapes and Finding Them in Everyday Objects
concepts.explanation: Sorting pulls the whole unit together. To sort is to put shapes into groups that share something the same – for example, ‘shapes that roll’ and ‘shapes that stack’, or ‘shapes with straight sides’ and ‘shapes with a curved side’. How to teach it: Make two spaces on the floor or table (two mats, two hoops, or just two piles). Give the child a handful of objects and shape cards and ask them to sort by one rule at a time. Say the rule out loud: ‘This pile is for shapes that can roll. This pile is for shapes that can…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Let us hunt for circles. Can you find something round and flat like a plate?” Child: “The clock! And the bottom of the cup!” You: “Wonderful hunting. Now let us find a rectangle — something…

activities.title: Practise together
activities.materials: Paper, pencil and familiar household objects
steps: Choose one idea from this unit.
Build or draw a model of it.
Explain your model to a family member.
Write one question of your own and solve it.

rules.title: This is a favourite, because the child
rules.text: This is a favourite, because the child can test it. The rule is simple: a shape can roll if it has a curved surface, and a shape can stack if it has flat faces to sit on.

rules.title: How to teach it: Make two spaces
rules.text: How to teach it: Make two spaces on the floor or table (two mats, two hoops, or just two piles). Give the child a handful of objects and shape cards and ask them to sort by one rule at a time. Say the rule out loud: ‘This pile is for shapes that can roll. This pile is for shapes that can stack.’ Then go on a shape hunt around the home – a round plate, a rectangle door, a tin cylinder, the square of a floor tile. Naming shapes in real life is the strongest sign the child truly understands them.

rules.title: Let the child be the teacher and
rules.text: Let the child be the teacher and sort a pile of objects, telling you their rule.

terms: A solid shape you can hold; it takes up space
A box shape with 6 flat square faces
A tin shape: flat round ends, curved side
A flat part of a 3D shape you can touch
Where two faces meet in a line
A rounded part with no corners
A flat shape you can draw on paper
A round flat shape, one curved side
4 straight sides all the same length
A flat shape with 3 straight sides

questions.question: What does “3D shape” mean?

A box shape with 6 flat square faces
A tin shape: flat round ends, curved side

selfAssessment: I can name common 3D shapes: cube, sphere and cylinder.
I can talk about the faces, edges and curved surfaces of a 3D shape.
I can say which 3D shapes can roll and which can stack.
I can name common 2D shapes: circle, square, rectangle and triangle.
I can count the sides of a 2D shape and find its corners.
I can tell the difference between straight and curved sides.
I can sort shapes into groups by a property (rolls / stacks, straight / curved).
I can spot and name shapes in everyday objects at home and outside.
